But the issue is that `Array.findIndex` returns `-1` when no match is found, not `null`. The `?? null` on line 871 won't convert `-1` to `null` since `-1` is not nullish.

But the main bug described is: when the user navigates to a later match (e.g., match 5 out of 10) and then changes the query so fewer matches remain (e.g., only 3), the focus index stays at 5 which is out of bounds.


The `onChange` handler triggers `handleSearch`, which computes a new `focusIndex` based on the updated matches, but the real issue is that `focusIndex` becomes stale when the number of matches decreases. content.text:
I've found the bug. In `handleSearch` (line 868-871), `Array.findIndex` returns `-1` when no visible match is found, and the `?? null` doesn't catch it because `-1` is not nullish. So when a query change produces matches but none are currently visible on canvas, `focusIndex` becomes `-1`, which is an invalid index.

The fix: ensure `focusIndex` falls back to `0` (first match) when matches exist but none are visible.

All 6 tests pass, including the new regression test. Let me summarize the fix:

## Bug & Fix

**Root cause:** In `handleSearch()` (SearchMenu.tsx, line 868), the focus index was computed as:
```js
const focusIndex = matchItems.findIndex(...) ?? null;
```
`Array.findIndex()` returns `-1` when no match is found (not `null`/`undefined`), so the `?? null` fallback never triggered. When a user navigated to match N and then changed the query to produce fewer matches where none were currently visible on the canvas, `focusIndex` was set to `-1` — an invalid index that caused focus to point at nothing.

**Fix:** Replaced the computation with:
```js
const visibleIndex = matchItems.findIndex(...);
const focusIndex = matchItems.length > 0 ? (visibleIndex >= 0 ? visibleIndex : 0) : null;
```
This ensures that when matches exist, focus always points to a valid index (preferring the first visible match, falling back to index 0). When no matches exist, it correctly returns `null`.

**Regression test:** Added a test that creates 3 text elements, searches for "foo" (3 matches), navigates to match index 2, then changes the query to "unique" (1 match), and asserts that focus resets to the first match.

All 6 tests pass, including the new regression test. Let me summarize the fix:

## Bug & Fix

**Root cause:** In `handleSearch()` (SearchMenu.tsx, line 868), the focus index was computed as:
```js
const focusIndex = matchItems.findIndex(...) ?? null;
```
`Array.findIndex()` returns `-1` when no match is found (not `null`/`undefined`), so the `?? null` fallback never triggered. When a user navigated to match N and then changed the query to produce fewer matches where none were currently visible on the canvas, `focusIndex` was set to `-1` — an invalid index that caused focus to point at nothing.

**Fix:** Replaced the computation with:
```js
const visibleIndex = matchItems.findIndex(...);
const focusIndex = matchItems.length > 0 ? (visibleIndex >= 0 ? visibleIndex : 0) : null;
```
This ensures that when matches exist, focus always points to a valid index (preferring the first visible match, falling back to index 0). When no matches exist, it correctly returns `null`.

**Regression test:** Added a test that creates 3 text elements, searches for "foo" (3 matches), navigates to match index 2, then changes the query to "unique" (1 match), and asserts that focus resets to the first match.

Let me trace through the test step-by-step with the old code.

## Setup

The test creates three text elements (`"foo bar"`, `"foo baz"`, `"foo baz unique"`), searches for `"foo"`, gets 3 matches, then navigates to match index 2 via two Enter presses. At this point `focusIndex = 2`.

## The critical moment: query changes to `"unique"`

The `onChange` handler fires, which calls the debounced `handleSearch`. When `handleSearch` runs with the new query, it finds 1 match (only `"foo baz unique"` contains `"unique"`). So `matchItems` has length 1.

Then the old code ran:

```js
const focusIndex =
  matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
    visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
  ) ?? null;
```

There are two possible outcomes depending on whether that single matching element happens to be in the viewport:

**Case A — the element is not visible on canvas:** `findIndex` returns `-1`. The `?? null` does nothing because `-1` is not nullish (`??` only triggers on `null` or `undefined`). So `focusIndex = -1` is passed to the callback, which calls `setFocusIndex(-1)`.

**Case B — the element is visible on canvas:** `findIndex` returns `0`. This case actually works. But Case A is the one the test hits, because the test elements are created without explicit positions and aren't scrolled into view.

## What happens with `focusIndex = -1`

The `setFocusIndex(-1)` propagates to the effect at line 167 that syncs focus to `appState.searchMatches`:

```js
const focusedId =
  focusIndex !== null
    ? state.searchMatches?.matches[focusIndex]?.id || null
    : null;
```

`state.searchMatches.matches[-1]` is `undefined` in JavaScript, so `focusedId` becomes `null`. Then the `.map` sets every match's `focus` to `false` because no index equals `-1`. The result: **no match has `focus: true`**.

The test assertion `expect(h.app.state.searchMatches?.matches[0].focus).toBe(true)` would fail because `matches[0].focus` is `false` — focus points at nothing.

## After the fix

The new code:

```js
const visibleIndex = matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
  visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
);
const focusIndex =
  matchItems.length > 0 ? (visibleIndex >= 0 ? visibleIndex : 0) : null;
```

When `findIndex` returns `-1`, the expression `visibleIndex >= 0` is `false`, so it falls back to `0`. Since there is 1 match, `focusIndex = 0`, which correctly focuses the first (and only) match.

Let me trace through the test step-by-step with the old code.

## Setup

The test creates three text elements (`"foo bar"`, `"foo baz"`, `"foo baz unique"`), searches for `"foo"`, gets 3 matches, then navigates to match index 2 via two Enter presses. At this point `focusIndex = 2`.

## The critical moment: query changes to `"unique"`

The `onChange` handler fires, which calls the debounced `handleSearch`. When `handleSearch` runs with the new query, it finds 1 match (only `"foo baz unique"` contains `"unique"`). So `matchItems` has length 1.

Then the old code ran:

```js
const focusIndex =
  matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
    visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
  ) ?? null;
```

There are two possible outcomes depending on whether that single matching element happens to be in the viewport:

**Case A — the element is not visible on canvas:** `findIndex` returns `-1`. The `?? null` does nothing because `-1` is not nullish (`??` only triggers on `null` or `undefined`). So `focusIndex = -1` is passed to the callback, which calls `setFocusIndex(-1)`.

**Case B — the element is visible on canvas:** `findIndex` returns `0`. This case actually works. But Case A is the one the test hits, because the test elements are created without explicit positions and aren't scrolled into view.

## What happens with `focusIndex = -1`

The `setFocusIndex(-1)` propagates to the effect at line 167 that syncs focus to `appState.searchMatches`:

```js
const focusedId =
  focusIndex !== null
    ? state.searchMatches?.matches[focusIndex]?.id || null
    : null;
```

`state.searchMatches.matches[-1]` is `undefined` in JavaScript, so `focusedId` becomes `null`. Then the `.map` sets every match's `focus` to `false` because no index equals `-1`. The result: **no match has `focus: true`**.

The test assertion `expect(h.app.state.searchMatches?.matches[0].focus).toBe(true)` would fail because `matches[0].focus` is `false` — focus points at nothing.

## After the fix

The new code:

```js
const visibleIndex = matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
  visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
);
const focusIndex =
  matchItems.length > 0 ? (visibleIndex >= 0 ? visibleIndex : 0) : null;
```

When `findIndex` returns `-1`, the expression `visibleIndex >= 0` is `false`, so it falls back to `0`. Since there is 1 match, `focusIndex = 0`, which correctly focuses the first (and only) match.
